data.title: Apartment Investment & Management Company: Unlocking A Concentrated Value Opportunity
data.description:
Apartment Investment & Management Company (NYSE:AIV) has not delivered the pivotal year hoped for when last covered on Seeking Alpha, generating negative YTD returns amidst rallies in U.S. equity indices. However, I believe their recent moves signal a willingness to fully liquidate their portfolio, which does not appear to be priced into the stock.

The Background

AIV owns the remaining assets from the AIRC spin-off in 2020 (then ~90% of their combined value), which went on to be acquired by Blackstone (BX) last year for about $10B. The REIT struggled to find natural owners without a dividend, with development assets that are notoriously hard to value. The stock has ping-ponged between $5 and $10, with corporate buybacks unable to close what management described as a significant discount to fair value. Ultimately, AIV has decided to pursue “alternatives” at the beginning of 2025, and this pursuit is ongoing.

The Catalyst

AIV recently closed on the sale of their suburban Boston properties, so far declaring a $2.23 per share special cash dividend and closing their final sale in Q4. This was in addition to the company's previously announced sale of two assets in Miami’s Brickell neighborhood for $520m, which remains on track to close in Q4-25.

The combined proceeds from all scheduled dispositions, including the Boston and Brickell sales, are expected to total $1.26B and generate approximately $785m in net proceeds after retiring associated debt. Management has committed to returning between $4-4.20 per share to stockholders from the total net proceeds, inclusive of the dividend mentioned above.

I believe the market is misinterpreting the Boston sale, and the stock sold off in its wake. First, these were very old properties with significant deferred maintenance, about an hour from Boston. Some were literally in New Hampshire. They were also sources of significant cash flow for the company, which was redeployed into development projects in the DC area and Florida. By selling their legacy cash-generating assets, the company is moving towards full liquidation, as their ability to self-fund development diminishes.

Estimating Remaining Value and Potential IRR

The primary value proposition for AIV lies in the significant discount between its share price and the Net Asset Value, or “NAV” of its properties. My assumptions for calculating the remaining NAV are as follows:

  • The company has already outlined expected Brickell/Boston proceeds treatment, including about $150m being withheld for corporate purposes and a $4-4.20 dividend.
  • Remaining Stabilized NOI: $51m, 6-6.5% aggregate cap rate expected offset by $430m recourse debt: $350-420m NAV

  • 2026 Stabilized NOI: $39m, 5.5-6% cap rate offset by $400m associated debt: $250-310m NAV
  • Miami 240 development carrying $165m debt: $35-50m NAV

  • $3m unconsolidated NOI: $40-50m NAV
  • Benson Hotel: $35-50m NAV
  • Remaining development properties & RE Tech assets: $150-200m NAV

  • Cash & notes receivable: I don’t believe the Brickell deposit cash is on the balance sheet already (so no double counting): $150m NAV
  • Total estimated NAV: $1,160 - $1,380m

Counting against these assets, we have:

  • $150m preferred stock liability
  • $150m other liabilities on balance sheet
  • Assume $50m liquidation/CIC costs
  • Net NAV: $810 - $1,030m

Added together, this gets you to a midpoint of $900m net distributable value over ~150m fully diluted shares (adjusting for GP conversion), or about $10-10.20/share before the expected dividends. Once the total cash distribution hits, the implied value of the remaining portfolio is deeply discounted relative to its estimated NAV:

  • Stock Price Before Distribution(s): $7.85/share
  • Total Expected Cash Return (Midpoint of $4.00-$4.20), $4.10/share
  • Author’s Estimated NAV (Pre-Distribution) $10/share
  • Estimated Remaining NAV (Post $4.10 Distribution): $5.90/share
  • Implied Price of Remaining Equity: $3.75/share
  • Potential Upside on Remaining Equity: 57%

I thought the stock price might recover more today after the first $2.23 dividend, but has traded down almost exactly in line with the payout, juicing the upside for new investors.

Risks

  • I may be wrong about the read-through from the recent Boston sale, with the remaining assets not being sold in the near term. I expect enough assets will be sold in the next twelve months to fully cover my cost basis.
  • Asset sale prices could underwhelm. AIV does not report asset management expenses in NOI, which has caused some confusion for investors on calculating cap rates on sale.
  • If the Brickell sale breaks, I would expect the stock price to decline. However, there were plenty of other rumored bidders on the site, and the non-refundable deposit should help bridge the gap to the next best bid.
  • For investors with taxable accounts, the dividends will be less efficient than those with tax-deferred retirement accounts. This likely limits the universe of investors considering AIV.
  • Rising interest rates could significantly impact the value of AIV's properties, whereas falling rates should present an opportunity for more upside than what I have shown here.

Conclusion

AIV looks attractive to me after recent sale announcements of their Boston portfolio and Brickell Assemblage. With one major dividend already paid out, the go-forward risk reward is looking significantly more attractive. I expect future asset sales announcements to cover my cost basis, with upside coming from sales of their remaining development projects. Disagree? Let me know why in the comments.

data.title: Rates Spark: Gilts Gaining Confidence
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker

US regional banking concerns spark renewed flight to safety

10y US Treasury yields dipped below 4% on Thursday. Also, 2y yields have slid to below 3.4%, falling beneath the levels just after 'Liberation Day,' hinting at growing expectations that the Fed might have to take rates below the neutral level. The trigger for the latest rates rally is concerns about hidden credit risks within the US regional banking sector; the equities index for the sector slid 6%, although it is not clear at this stage to what degree this is a broader systemic issue or just markets jumping on headlines for lack of other drivers.

Over the past few weeks, the US shutdown has already weighed on sentiment, especially given the stories about larger layoffs in the federal workforce amid general worries about the job market. On the other hand, the shutdown means there were not many big directional impulses from the data side. So what had been missing was a good reason to get below 4% in the 10y, but concerns surrounding regional banks are now the new input.

Apart from the downward drift also to EUR rates, the renewed flight to quality has clear implications for the German Bund, which is proving its role as a safe haven with yields dipping further below swaps.

Gilts winning this week reflecting confidence in November budget

The big winner this week is gilts, with the 10Y yield down some 20bp, but with the autumn budget on our doorstep, we should brace for more volatility. Whilst gilts are strongly correlated with US Treasuries, they haven't followed the drift lower in US yields of the past few months. Instead, they remain stubbornly anchored around 4.6%. One key reason is that the Bank of England, unlike the Fed, is not in full cutting mode.

Another reason for still elevated yields is the risk premium embedded in gilts, but we think this should ease after we have more budget certainty. The spread between gilt yields and the swap rate currently stands at 62bp, which is now above the UST swap spread of 48bp. More surprisingly, gilt spreads have most recently risen in line with the widening of French government bond spreads but did not tighten alongside UST and Bunds. In fact, over the past year, gilt spreads were much more correlated with Italian government bond spreads than with US Treasuries or Bunds.

Whilst we acknowledge that the UK is facing fiscal challenges, we do think gilt yields should find themselves lower over the medium term. Our estimate of a 20bp risk premium versus 10Y US Treasuries still seems excessive, especially given that US government finances are under significant pressure. Once we get more certainty about the budget in November, the volatility in gilts should ease, which would then help the risk premium compress. We therefore see this week's decline in yields as a justified move, and there's still room for more.

Gilts were recently more aligned with French than German bonds

Friday’s events and market views

A light day in terms of data. From the eurozone, we only expect final CPI numbers for September, but these don't tend to change much. And due to the government shutdown, we don't anticipate any notable data from the US. In terms of central bank speakers, we have the Bank of England's Pill, Greene and Breeden talking later. From the European Central Bank, we have Nagel and Rehn, and from the Fed, Musalem.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: CareCloud 8.75% CUM RED PERP PFD SER B: Not Your Usual Preferred Shares
data.description:
Thesis

Preferred shares are usually considered fixed income instruments, although they do not sit on the 'Liabilities' part of the balance sheet. Investors use these capital markets instruments for income, with little upside present in the instruments. In most cases preferred shares are driven by interest rate risk, but occasionally they also see a significant impact from credit risk. It is the case for the CareCloud 8.75% Series B Preferred Shares (NASDAQ:CCLDO), which are preferred shares issued by CareCloud Inc. (NASDAQ:CCLD).

In today's article we are going to explore CCLD's capital structure and financials, and show investors why CCLDO are not your usual fixed income instrument, but rather represents a take on the company's future and profitability.

What does CareCloud do?

CareCloud is a healthcare technology company that provides a suite of cloud-based, technology-enabled solutions for medical practices and health systems. The company acts as a back office for healthcare businesses by handling billing and collections, managing patient data, and managing day to day scheduling and other tasks.

The company is fairly small, with a market capitalization of $135 million and total assets on the balance sheet of only $75 million.

Financial history

Despite its small size, the name has seen a fairly robust increase in revenue figures throughout time:

After a significant growth from 2019 to 2022, revenues have stagnated or even receded. The company is trying to rectify that via its Medsphere Systems Corporation acquisition, which just closed in August 2025. This acquisition is meant to expand CareCloud's reach into the hospital IT market, particularly for small and mid-sized hospitals.

The company's net income is again turning positive, after a number of years of negative figures:

More importantly, the management team's guidance is encouraging after its strategic acquisition:

That being said, there is another large reason for the positive outlook - the reduction in dividends from paying preferred shares. While the company has virtually no liabilities, it does spend roughly $12 million per year in dividend payments to preferred shares. That is why CCLD retired this year its Series A preferred shares via a conversion to common stock:

SOMERSET, N.J., March 06, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- CareCloud, Inc. (the “Company”) (Nasdaq: CCLD, CCLDO, CCLDP), a leader in healthcare information technology and generative AI solutions for medical practices and health systems nationwide, announced today (the “Mandatory Exchange Date”) that it effected the mandatory conversion (the "Conversion") of shares of its 8.75% Series A Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred Stock (the “Preferred Stock”), into the Company’s Common Stock, $0.001 par value per share (the “Common Stock”). “We are excited to announce this conversion, which will eliminate approximately $7 million or more in annual dividend obligations, freeing us to reinvest this capital in our growth,” said Norman Roth, Interim Chief Financial Officer and Corporate Controller of CareCloud.

We can see the immediate impact to net income in the latest quarter:

For the three months ended June 30, 2025 the preferred stock dividend moved to $1.3 million from $3.9 million in the same period in 2024.

Not traditional fixed income

As we have said before, traditional preferred equity investments rarely experience very substantial drawdowns, unless the underlying company is about to default. CCLDO however, has performed differently due to several factors:

  • small company with less financial flexibility
  • absence of other significant liabilities, with capital coming from preferred equity and common equity only
  • lack of a history of constant profitability

If we have a look at CCLDO's performance we will understand exactly what that means:

The preferred shares had a -85% drawdown in December 2023 when the company decided to suspend interest payment on the shares due to poor operating results. Furthermore the company took a $42 million goodwill impairment charge for that quarter, which moved its net income to deep negative territory.

Please note the correlation between the common shares and the preferred shares:

We rarely see such correlation, with the common equity being the first loss piece in a medium sized company. Due to size though, both the common and preferred shares move together when net income figures are below expectations. This should not occur, and does not occur, for a larger more established enterprise.

Yield considerations

The Series B comes with a coupon of 8.75%, but its current yield is 10%. We think this is too low. Fixed income instruments do not have -85% drawdowns without some sort of restructuring, and the fact that the upside is capped here but the downside is the same as common stock should command a higher coupon in our view.

When one invests in preferred equity one gets paid a coupon. The upside is limited by the $25/share par value, and even if the business does very well, all that benefit goes to common shareholders. Given the risk/rewards for CCLDO, one would expect to see current yields closer to 15%-20%, similar to what an investor gets via CLO equity CEFs. As we have seen from its historic performance, CCLDO acts like pure equity; thus, it should be priced as such.

We are of the opinion that investors looking for upside should buy the CCLD common equity, while traditional fixed income investors should look for either higher yields from CCLDO or avoid it altogether. CCLDO is not your typical fixed income investment.

Conclusion

CCLDO represents the Series B preferred shares from CareCloud Inc. The company is very small, with a market cap below $150 million, and has transformed its Series A preferred shares into common equity earlier in 2025. CCLDO had a -85% drawdown in 2023/2024 as CareCloud was not performing in line with expectations. The preferred shares are not a traditional fixed income investment, but represent more of a bet on the survival of the business, but without any of the upside of the common equity. Investors looking at CareCloud should look at the common stock, while traditional fixed income investors would be well-served by avoiding this volatile security.

data.title: Ryman Hospitality Properties: Upscale Hotels And Entertainment Assets
data.description:
Ryman Hospitality Properties (NYSE:RHP) ownership of upscale and meeting-focused destination hotels in a range of urban and resort markets in the U.S. has provided the REIT with healthy earnings visibility and a clear pathway for dividend growth as the Fed looks set to push through another 50 basis points of rate cuts to end 2025. However, while I've been adding more shares, near-term volatility is to be expected as elevated inflation expectations keep the yield on longer-term Treasuries high to dampen demand for REITs that are broadly now trading closer to their 52-week low. I'm in pursuit of income, with RHP last declaring a quarterly cash dividend of $1.15 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution, and $4.6 per share annualized for a 5.09% dividend yield. This means a spread of around 111 basis points above the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y). RHP's credit rating was most recently affirmed at "BB-" by Fitch Ratings. The play here is that RHP should be able to maintain a 4% to 6% compound annual growth rate far into the future, and that the REIT should see valuation eventually as the Fed's rate-cutting cycle is enacted. I last covered RHP last year, with the commons dipping since then in response to the Fed keeping rates broadly elevated up until the recent September rate cut.

Entertainment Growth, FFO Dip, And Dividend Coverage

RHP generated fiscal 2025 second-quarter revenue of $659.51 million, up 7.5% over its year-ago comp and a beat by $41.15 million on consensus estimates. Growth was driven by the REIT's entertainment operating segment, Opry Entertainment Group's January acquisition of a majority interest in Southern Entertainment ("SE"). SE's portfolio includes a range of owned and promoted music festivals, including the Barefoot Country Music Fest in New Jersey's Wildwood, the Carolina Country Music Fest in Myrtle Beach, and an upcoming Field & Stream Music Fest in Winnsboro, South Carolina. However, the Field & Stream Music Fest was cancelled for the second year in a row, with full refunds being issued for tickets and other add-ons. The cancellation was likely due to low ticket sales. It represents a setback for RHP, as festivals are incredibly seasonal, and the 2025 Field & Stream Music Fest, slotted for the first week of October, would have acted to boost RHP's fourth-quarter earnings.

The REIT generated second-quarter funds from operations ("FFO") of $2.14 per share, down 43 cents from $2.57 a year ago, on the back of higher total operating expenses and interest expenses. Total operating expenses were up 16.8% year-over-year to $520 million, a rate of growth that came in ahead of total revenue growth. Food and beverage was the most disappointing line item, with a year-over-year decline in sales realized during the second quarter as its relevant expenses were up $3.8 million over the same time frame. Crucially, entertainment now forms 21.7% of total revenue, up 636 basis points from 15.36% in the year-ago period. This unit includes concerts, music-inspired restaurants, digital content, and the Grand Ole Opry, a live music venue in Nashville. The REIT is still covering its dividend by 186% through its second-quarter FFO and is guiding for adjusted FFO for its full-year fiscal 2025 to come in at $8.21 per share at the midpoint for end-of-year dividend coverage of around 178% on an adjusted basis.

RHP's acquisition of JW Marriott Phoenix Desert Ridge for $865 million is also set to be accretive to FFO from next year, with the REIT partially financing the purchase through an upsized registered public offering of around 2.6 million shares at $96.20 per share. Desert Ridge will add 950 rooms to RHP's portfolio and comes with a higher average daily rate ("ADR") of $349 and a revenue per available room ("RevPAR") of $230 than the other upscale properties in RHP's portfolio. The focus on a niche and high-RevPAR hospitality portfolio, country music entertainment venues, and upscale convention center resorts places RHP as one of the more unique REITs in my portfolio and a source of broad diversification. The depth of coverage and pending Fed rate cuts should aggregate to support a dividend raise next year. The CME FedWatch Tool currently has the probability of 50 basis points of cuts to end 2025 at 86%.

Conclusion

RHP is set to report its third-quarter earnings after the market closes on Monday, November 3, 2025. This should see a full quarter of contributions from a summer of festivals from its entertainment unit, and I expect third-quarter revenue to grow sequentially. The REIT's portfolio is set for strength, but the year-over-year dip in FFO seen during the second quarter and operating expenses that were higher versus their year-ago quarter act to counter bullishness on this. The Gaylord Hotels account for 5 of the top 10 non-gaming hotels in the U.S. by indoor meeting space, and RHP's inclusion of Desert Ridge in this should form an engine for growth. RHP is being rated as a hold.

data.title: American Eagle Outfitters: Short-Lived Hype, Stock Is Too Expensive, Sell
data.description:
Intro

American Eagle Outfitters (NYSE:AEO) is an American lifestyle retailer specializing in casual apparel, footwear, and accessories, primarily for young adults (or those who still feel young). Founded in 1977 by brothers Jerry and Mark Silverman, AEO began as a subsidiary of Retail Ventures, Inc., offering brand-name leisure gear for outdoor pursuits. Rebranded in 1991 amid financial challenges, AEO shifted to private-label merchandise for differentiation. It went public in February 1994, raising funds for growth.

Today, AEO is renowned for brands like American Eagle (core denim and tops), Aerie (intimates and activewear), and Offline by Aerie (athleisure), with over 1,000 global stores blending affordability and trendiness. This year, AEO made headlines through an ad campaign with American actress Sydney Sweeney that was said to have pulled in plenty of new customers over the summer.

However, the initial hype and the stock's massive 25% pop post earnings have reversed. I rate AEO stock a "sell", as the underlying fundamentals are not strong enough to justify the current valuation.

Weaker Fundamentals Than Thought

The long-term chart of AEO shows us we are not dealing with a growing company or a long-term success story.

A price return of 1,196% might sound nice, but this is over 30 years. I doubt the majority of investors have been holding so long. Else, we can see that AEO stock currently is trading on a level where it was in the mid-2000s. Hardly impressive. So basically, 20 years of no returns (excluding the dividend). But plenty of volatility and price fluctuation.

This doesn't mean one cannot make a few bucks with such a stock. But it depends on the entry point. This can be determined at least approximately through a fundamental analysis, which is what we're doing today.

Zooming in, over the last decade, long-term investors would have made only 6% (before dividends), an unsatisfying outcome. As said, in between there can be lucrative entry points in this cyclical company, which is driven by discretionary consumer spending, but also the taste of consumers within the business cycle.

After this foreword, let's examine the company's financial performance. Sales are showing a long-term upward trend. So fine, so good.

Sales growth rates are a bit trickier. Broadly speaking, on average sales are growing somewhere in the low- to mid-single digits with fluctuations. FY 22 was exceptional, as we know, and I would under no circumstances use such a year as a yardstick for future growth assumptions.

Looking now at sales on a rolling-twelve-month quarterly basis, we see the direction is shifting. Sales seem to have peaked for now, as the last few quarters have been showing falling sales. These are all last-twelve-month numbers.

If we now pull out the sales growth rates, we see momentum has shifted to the downside.

We can debate whether this is due to tariffs, more cautious consumers when it comes to discretionary items, both, or something else. But at the moment, there's no growth, and the stock should reflect that, which, for my taste, is not doing (coming back to the valuation later).

Even the guidance from management does not see any growth, despite the hype around the Sydney Sweeney ad and the stock's pop after the last results were released in early April.

Latest management guidance:

Sales are expected to be flat for the year, while gross margins are trending down. Higher sales expenses (SG&A), little surprisingly, but together with tariff-induced higher costs (reflected in gross margins), lead the management to expect operating income of $255–$265 million. This is not a great expected outcome, as I will show now.

First, gross margins. I have to admit, management was able to keep them relatively stable. They have even risen modestly over the last ten years from start to finish.

But with the exception of the crisis years 2020–2021, the expected operating income of $260 million at the midpoint, would be the lowest level over the last rolling decade. Yes, I know, tariff-induced due to higher costs.

But for me, it looks like management tries to keep sales up through expensive ad campaigns. According to them, promotions have been lower thanks to these successful campaigns. But in the end, both are marketing expenses, so that in practice it is from the left pocket to the right pocket.

Anyhow, net income has been rather lumpy in the past. This confirms AEO is not a straight-line growth story.

Taking a look at cash flow, we can see again at best a slow-growth story.

The most important figure for me, free cash flow, gives us even fewer reasons to celebrate.

Only little growth and the business remains very capital intensive, as Capex eats big (and growing) parts of operating cash flow.

You might be wondering, why I am so negative, despite relatively solid looking numbers. I am answering this question now. The reason is the more time passes, the less efficient the company becomes.

For example, returns on assets and capital. Both are trending down. Definitely not positive.

Then, inventory turnover is trending down either. This means, AEO becomes less and less dynamic, with its clothes remaining in stock longer each year. Over the last twelve months, by the way, this figure for the first time dropped below 5x to 4.8x.

This is confirmed by rising average days sales and inventory outstanding. If demand were so strong, these metrics would not be developing as they do.

In other words, the company needs to invest more and more capital to keep the wheel spinning, while efficiency declines. Paired with a lumpy growth outlook in the currently tough environment, the stock should be valued at a discount.

AEO has only a limited amount of debt, which is an advantage. Also, goodwill and intangibles are low, with total equity strongly above these figures. The balance sheet is really strong.

Which leads us to the valuation.

Valuation

Over the last quarters, AEO has aggressively repurchased shares. Especially in the last quarter, management stepped on the gas (likely in anticipation of a successful outcome of their ad campaign).

During the first half, 10% of the stock was bought back. This is very significant.

However, this move has noticeably drained the cash reserves.

In the short term, this looks like a genius move. Below, I framed the period where the buyback likely was conducted. As we can see, at much lower prices. Then the stock popped after the results announcement.

However, AEO stock has already lost a good quarter from its high. This is not making headlines. If the stock dives another 10%, the entire pop that followed the earnings release will be gone. Just to put things into perspective.

Another point is, management spent more than their entire FCF generated over the last twelve months for this buyback. So this was a very aggressive move. But it seems the bullets are fired now. I hope they don't borrow money for another round of buybacks.

The dividend, yielding 3.3%, consumes $90 million annually at current figures. So effectively, AEO generated only $60 million in excess cash after subtracting the dividend from FCF.

I am skeptical, as management isn't expecting to grow this year with cost pressure from tariffs. Okay, they can hike prices to offset or at least counterbalance this a bit. But I doubt consumers will be storming into the stores when prices get jacked up strongly. The current environment is tough to navigate. We have seen, for example, even consumer staples like food producers having issues raising their prices. Clothes are being bought less frequently and are easier to trade down (or wear longer). If they need to be purchased at all, most of us have more in stock than we are wearing. I am not so sure AEO will be able to increase prices strongly without causing side ripple effects in the form of falling demand.

With a FCF of $150 million over the last twelve months and a market cap of $2.6 billion, the multiple is rather steep at 17x. Cash flow was suppressed by negative working capital. Adjusting for that, FCF would be $260 million—a 10x multiple. This sounds more like fair value to me than a dramatic undervaluation.

Where my decision shifted from "hold" to "sell" was a PE ratio comparison. AEO trades for a forward PE of 14x.

For me, this is way too much for this business, despite the recent hype about the ad campaign. In comparison, The GAP (GAP) is trading for a forward PE of 10x, which I deem to be more realistic. The dividend yield is similar, GAP even has a modestly better balance sheet (decent net cash position), significantly more liquidity on hand, firmer margins, and better cash generation. If at all, GAP should trade at a premium, not AEO.

Such apparel retailers should not be bought for more than 10x earnings/FCF, assuming a clean balance sheet. Ideally below that, when they are in a cyclical trough. This is not the case currently. AEO is being priced too optimistically for my taste.

That's why I rate it a "sell", expecting the stock to come back at least to where it was before the pop post Q2 earnings. I fully acknowledge that AEO now has significantly fewer shares outstanding. But please see what the market has been thinking about AEO over the long term. Despite growing sales, the trend for the whole market cap is rather down.

Until the risk and reward does not tilt to a much more favorable setup, I rate AEO a "sell". The risks are too high for me to catch a value trap on its way down in a challenging consumer environment. I view the recent hype as being rather a one-off event than a major turnaround.

Risks To My Thesis

AEO could be acquired by a bigger competitor or taken private, as the market cap is only $2.6 billion, with very little net debt on the balance sheet.

If I am wrong and the recent ad campaign indeed ignited significant new interest in the company and its brands, the business could beat expectations, justifying the current valuation. But this assumes some growth and higher margins with strong and stable cash generation.

Conclusion

I rate AEO a "sell" as the stock looks too expensive for me. Recent hype or not, most of the post-earnings pop has been erased again. The business is becoming less and less efficient over time, making me cautious.

data.title: 3 Beaten-Down Stocks Ready To Rally
data.description:
Earnings Buoy Indexes as Rare Earths Rhetoric Heats Up

Major averages continue to trade near all-time highs, with the S&P 500 (SPY) hitting a new record on October 8. The drivers behind the sustained rally have been twofold: the ongoing AI boom has fueled gains across the supply chain, while expectations that the Fed will cut interest rates in October have reinforced its momentum. Currently, 97% of traders expect a rate cut at the October FOMC meeting.

Expectations for a rate cut in October are widespread

Source Link: CME FedWatch

Notably, fund managers’ optimism toward stocks has reached its highest point since February, while recession fears have dropped to their lowest level in over three years, according to Bank of America’s Global Fund Manager Survey.

Market optimism is at its highest point since February

Source Link

However, several risks to market momentum lurk just beneath the surface. Last Friday, indexes reeled when President Trump threatened new tariffs on Chinese imports in response to strict export controls on rare earths. The news triggered a sell-off, marking the worst day for the market since April and hinted at an underlying fragility in the risk-on rally. While solid earnings results have helped steady markets in the interim, the protracted government shutdown could do lasting damage to the economy. Declining Treasury yields and a rally in gold prices are further evidence of growing caution among investors.

Gold continues its historic rally

Source Link: Bloomberg

Stretched valuations leave investors vulnerable to sudden downturns. By rotating into high-quality companies, particularly those that have retreated amid the broader exuberance, investors can capture long-term upside potential at a reasonable price.

3 Overlooked Stocks Ready for a Rally

Using Seeking Alpha’s Ratings Screener, I identified stocks that have pulled back despite strong ‘Strong Buy’ ratings. Three names spanning sectors and industries emerged, and I drilled into their underlying factor grades to get a better sense of their fundamentals. All three of these selections display excellent growth and EPS revisions grades, as well as discounted valuations and excellent profitability. For investors seeking a more systematic edge, I recommend Alpha Picks or the PRO Quant Portfolio to harness Seeking Alpha’s Quant model, which can deliver curated stock selections and portfolio insights.

3 Overlooked Stocks Showcase Solid Fundamentals

Below, we’ll outline the selections in more detail.

1. Willdan Group, Inc. (WLDN)

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Industry: Research and Consulting Services

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/16/2025): 16 out of 619

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/16/2025): 1 out of 39

  • Market Capitalization: $1.41B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

In the past month, energy and infrastructure solutions provider Willdan has pulled back from its highs due to more cautious investor sentiment and a wave of profit-taking after a phenomenal run earlier in the summer. This combination of factors has tempered its previous rally, despite excellent fundamentals, which is why WLDN is a member of the Alpha Picks portfolio that delivers two data-driven stocks each month using powerful Quant Ratings.

Source Link: WLDN Q2 2025 Investor Presentation

Willdan delivered record quarterly results, with 31% net revenue growth and expanding margins, driven mostly by surging demand from data center projects and new contract wins. The company’s energy segment, which accounts for roughly 85% of revenues, has benefited from AI-infrastructure tailwinds, helping to drive optimistic full-year guidance. The company sports excellent growth, supported by FWD EPS that is 680% above the industrials sector and 52% operating cash flow growth.

WLDN Growth Grade

WLDN boasts solid profitability, which has improved from a ‘C’ grade three months ago to its current ‘B-’ status. Highlights include a levered free cash flow margin of 9.49% and an ROE of 14.68% which are 40% and nearly 20% above the industrials sector, respectively. From a valuation perspective, the recent pullback has only augmented its appeal; WLDN maintains the same valuation grade that it did six months ago despite returning 147% in the same period. The company’s powerful growth, disciplined capital management, and healthy contract pipeline reinforce its future potential and status as an overlooked stock ready to rally.

2. Blue Bird Corporation (BLBD)

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Industry: Construction Machinery & Heavy Transportation Equipment

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/16/2025): 24 out of 619

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/16/2025): 2 out of 29

  • Market Capitalization: $1.81B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

Blue Bird Corporation has experienced a share price retreat following a post-earnings run-up in August, with lingering tariff headwinds and rumors of clean energy funding cuts weighing on investor sentiment. However, the company’s robust 3,900-unit backlog and record-level margins demonstrate resilience in the face of these potential policy headwinds.

Source Link: FY 2025 Q3 Financial Results

The company showcases promising forward-looking growth characteristics, including a FWD EPS Growth that is 876% above the sector median, as well as 148% levered free cash flow growth. BLBD has successfully employed a margin-neutral tariff strategy, reflected in the company’s ‘A-’ Profitability Grade, undergirded by a 65% ROE. This has resulted in a universally bullish sentiment from analysts, with five FY1 up revisions in the last 90 days versus zero downwards.

BLBD Revisions Grade

In addition to being attractively valued, trading 34% below the sector median in terms of its FWD P/E, BLBD has initiated a $100M share buyback program over the next two years. This program is supported by the company’s powerful free cash flow generation and is just one of several attractive attributes for investors looking to capitalize on a beaten-down stock.

3. Fresh Del Monte Produce Inc. (FDP)

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Industry: Agricultural Products & Services

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 10/16/2025): 8 out of 179

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 10/16/2025): 2 out of 13

  • Market Capitalization: $1.61B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

FDP, a leading global producer and distributor of fruits and vegetables, has struggled over the last month partly due to sector headwinds. The risk-on sentiment that has characterized markets has negatively impacted the Consumer Staples sector, which is flat MTD. However, recent volatility presents a buying opportunity for the stock, which showcases exceptional fundamentals.

FDP vs. the S&P 500 vs. the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLP) One Month Return

Despite strongly outperforming the sector over the last year (+12.45% vs. 3.53%), FDP’s valuation remains compelling. The company trades at a discount across nearly all its underlying metrics, including a 69% discount in terms of its P/B ratio.

FDP Valuation Grade

FDP has benefited from rising pineapple demand, which is slated to be a major growth driver for the company in the future.

“We see a very strong pineapple market going forward, especially with our premium varieties that is unparalleled in the market. And our expertise and our seeds are definitely better than anything in the market… we are already expanding our production capacity, and we expect there will be ... a meaningful increase in our production in the next 2 to 3 years, ” said Mohammad Abu-Ghazaleh, Chairman & CEO of FDP.

From a Quant perspective, the company showcases excellent growth characteristics, including a Y/Y operating cash flow growth of 4.82% vs. the sector’s -3.77%. The stock, while well-positioned for capital appreciation, also offers a strong dividend, with a 3.57% FWD yield. Supported by diversified global demand and strong fundamentals, FDP could be a strong option for investors looking to diversify into consumer staples with a strong dividend profile.

Quality Names Worth a Second Look

Optimism continues to pervade markets, driven by the AI boom and expectations of Fed rate cuts. However, several risks pose a threat to the continued risk-on rally. Last Friday's sharp sell-off following Trump's tariff threats marked the worst day since April, while the government shutdown, declining Treasury yields, and rallying gold prices signal growing investor caution. With valuations stretched, investors remain vulnerable to sudden downturns. Stacking up on high-quality companies that have retreated amid the broader exuberance offers a way to capture long-term upside potential.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using stock screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Consider using Seeking Alpha's 'Ratings Screener' tool to help find stocks that achieve diversification into the desired sectors you like.

data.title: Precious Metals Hit Another Record High
data.description:
By Ewa Manthey & Warren Patterson

Metals – Precious metals hit another record high

Gold and silver hit another record high this morning, topping the all-time peak set just yesterday, with spot prices jumping to $4,379.93/oz and $54.4/0z, respectively. The rally is driven by concerns over economic credit quality, expectations of continued Fed easing, and escalating US-China trade tensions. Two regional banks disclosed loan issues tied to alleged fraud, raising fears about weakening borrower credit, boosting safe-haven demand.

Meanwhile, traders are increasing their bets on a jumbo US rate cut by year-end, while Fed Chair Powell signalled another quarter-point reduction is likely to happen this month. Additionally, the US government shutdown has delayed key economic data releases, but once resolved, a surge of information could reveal signs of weakness, supporting further rate cuts.

Gold and silver are one of the best-performing commodities this year, with prices up by more than 65% and 80% YTD, respectively, supported by central bank purchases, strong ETF inflows, rising demand for safe-haven assets amid geopolitical tensions and concerns over the Fed’s independence.

Energy – Oil declines on rising US inventories

Oil prices were approaching a third consecutive weekly decline, with WTI falling to the lowest since May this morning, following a bearish US oil inventory report. Recent reports suggest that President Trump will meet with his Russian counterpart Vladimir Putin to discuss ending the war in Ukraine, partially easing fears of further destruction of the Russian energy infrastructure.

US inventory data from the Energy Information Administration (EIA) shows that crude oil inventories increased for a third straight week by 3.5m barrels over the last week, as production increased while refinery capacity use fell from the week before. However, this was lower compared to the 7.36m barrels increase that the American Petroleum Institute (API) reported the previous day. Total oil stocks stood at 423.8m barrels (the highest since early September), 4% below the five-year average. Meanwhile, crude stocks at Cushing fell by 703k barrels for a third consecutive week to 22m barrels over the reporting week, the lowest since mid-July. Crude imports fell by 878k b/d to 5.5m b/d, while exports rose by 876k b/d to 4.5m b/d.

For refined products, gasoline stocks fell marginally by 267k barrels, slightly higher than the average market expectation of a draw of just 111.4k barrels. Distillate stocks declined significantly by 4.5m barrels, compared to the market expecting a fall of 212.8k barrels. Furthermore, refinery utilisation fell by 6.7pp WoW to 85.7% over the reporting period.

Singapore refined product inventories rose significantly by 3.14m barrels WoW to 48.4m barrels (the highest since 17 September) for the week ending on 15 October 2025. The addition was driven by light distillates and residuals, which increased by 2.1m barrels and 1.4m barrels to 13.6m barrels and 25.1m barrels, respectively. However, middle distillate stocks fell marginally by 400k barrels to 9.7m barrels over the reporting week.

In Europe, refined product inventories in the ARA region increased by 56kt WoW to 6.03mt for the week ending on 16 October 2025, according to the latest data from Insights Global. The increase was driven by fuel oil, naphtha and gasoline stocks rising by 40kt, 20kt and 4kt respectively. However, gasoil inventories fell by 55kt to 1.13kt over the reporting week. Both sets of numbers suggest that middle distillates remain comfortable in these regions.

US natural gas prices slid to the lowest in three weeks this morning, with NYMEX Henry Hub futures falling to the intraday lows of $2.9/MMBtu. Prices declined for a fourth consecutive session today, following continued weekly storage injections largely offsetting the forecasts for cooler weather across the eastern half of the US in late October. EIA weekly gas storage data shows that US gas inventory rose by 80Bcf last week, in line with the average market expectation for a build of around 80.8Bcf. This was also close to the five-year average addition of 83Bcf for this time of year. Total gas stockpiles totalled 3.721Tcf as of 10 October, which is 4.3% above the five-year average. Inventories are all set to start the upcoming winter at a surplus.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Armanino Foods of Distinction: Upgrading To Buy On Revenue And Margin Gains
data.description:
Intro

We last wrote on Armanino Foods of Distinction, Inc. (OTCPK:AMNF) in June of 2024 when we doubled down on our 'Hold' rating in the international food company. Armanino produces a wide range of offerings, from frozen pestos in a variety of different flavours (Basil, roasted garlic, etc), varying sauces, pastas, and meatballs. Due to supply-chain limitations at the time (limiting Armanino's ability to get its products out the door), we assumed the absence of temporary top-line growth would eventually take its toll on Armanino stock. How wrong we were.

20-Year Chart

In fact, shares have returned approximately 92% over the past 16 months, which is an excellent performance considering the S&P has increased approximately 22% over the same time frame. Furthermore, despite the strong rally in recent quarters, the mistake here may be in believing that the stock is overvalued at its present juncture. In fact, if we pull up a long-term 20-year chart of Armanino, we see the stock in past times has been much further above its multi-year bullish trendline, which we depict below. Many times, established trends (the long-term ADX trend-following indicator continues to rise) have a higher probability of continuing than reversing, all things remaining equal. Therefore, from perusing through the company's latest quarterly report (released 8/8/2025) and most recent annual report, we are upgrading our rating in AMNF to a 'Buy' until further notice.

Margins Continue To Go From Strength To Strength

Many investors make the mistake of believing that when a stock rises, its valuation automatically becomes overvalued. However, what about the contrary, when share-price growth may be underperforming growth rates in the likes of the company's revenues and earnings? Then, you have a situation where, despite a company experiencing significant share-price gains, its stock may still be undervalued from a valuation perspective.

In the second quarter this year, reported sales of $19,970 million ended up being a 14.2% increase over the same period of 12 months prior. Gross profit accelerated to $9.106 million (25.2% rolling year growth), resulting in GAAP income of $4.85 million for the quarter (27.5% growth). EPS growth (30%) actually outperformed GAAP profit growth due to management continuing to bring down the float.

Revenues & Profits Expanding In Tandem

Many times, when a company is expanding, margins take a hit in the near term (due to absorption of fresh costs), but we see nothing of the sort in AMNF. With growth continuing to come from reaching more customers in international and foodservice, Armanino has been benefiting from lower prices from providers, where favourable commodity pricing has also played into the bullish picture here.

Can the current dynamic continue? There is no reason to believe otherwise, as manufacturing capacity continues to be scaled to keep up with demand. The question is whether the company's sales and earnings will swiftly follow suit, but recent trends, as we have seen above, demonstrate that strong growth should continue unabated. In fact, notwithstanding the continued potential of international markets, the US market is far from being saturated, which is why we believe Armanino's brands will continue to gain traction, all things remaining equal.

Suffice it to say, if revenues keep growing aggressively, and margins continue their upward trend, share-price appreciation is a foregone conclusion. The CEO pointed to the same in her comments below

Our long-term strategy is clear: continue growing a high-margin, capital-efficient business anchored in strong customer relationships, operational excellence, and category leadership. With a resilient model and a strong track record of execution, we are confident in our ability to deliver sustained performance and meaningful value for our shareholders over the long term.”

Earnings Remain Cheap Versus Sector Median

Strong Q2 gains mean the company's trailing GAAP profit comes in at $17.1 million. This means Armanino's trailing P/E comes in at 18.28, demonstrating that the company's earnings are trading at a 10% discount to the sector median (P/E of 21.18). We acknowledge that Armanino's sales (P/S) and assets (P/B) come in well above the sector medians, but Armanino is far more profitable, and this is the chief leveler here.

Recent gains mean that Armanino's GAAP profit margin now comes in at an elevated 23.3% over a trailing 12-month average. This profit margin provides a significant buffer against a potential softening of the restaurant sector, for example, unlike many of its competitors (4.67% sector median). Case in point, downturns many times provide ample opportunities for cash-rich companies with excellent earnings profiles and strong balance sheets to gain market share aggressively.

Return On Capital Strength

Furthermore, from a profitability standpoint, if we were just to focus on the capital that Armanino currently uses to generate its profits, we would have a much higher return on capital, all things remaining equal. Our formula for adjusted ROC = GAAP Profit / (Net Assets) + (Working Capital - Cash).

Now, working capital at the end of Q2 dropped slightly to $25.7 million due to share buybacks and increasing dividend payments. GAAP profit over a trailing 12-month basis came in at $17.1 million, net assets at $8.38 million, and cash amounted to $23 million. Plugging the above numbers into our adjusted ROC formula above results in an adjusted ROC of 154%.

The above exercise really hammers home how well management of this company continues to allocate capital. Furthermore, the result points to very strong brand recognition, sustained free cash flow generation, and resilience if indeed the company's markets were to contract any time soon.

Risks

Being able to keep 'costs of goods sold' suppressed would be difficult if areas such as tariffs, supplier relationships, or commodity pricing were to upset the applecart, so to speak. Furthermore, what has been hanging over this stock for some time now is the fact that one distributor in the US makes up over half of Armanino's gross sales. This obviously brings concentration risk into play, but the power of the company's brand would ultimately drive demand back in-store over time. The most recent annual report points to this below

The loss of distributors who represent a significant amount of sales could have a materially adverse effect on the business of the Company. However, the Company believes that once distributors have established accounts with customers such as supermarket chains, the termination of a distributor will not generally affect sales to such customers when another distributor serving the area is available, or the Company can take over marketing responsibilities.

Conclusion

Therefore, to sum up, we are upgrading Armanino Foods of Distinction, Inc. to a 'Buy' due to strong top-line growth trends, margin gains, and excellent profitability both from a GAAP and return on capital standpoint. Let's see what Q3 numbers deliver. We look forward to continued coverage.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tutor Perini: Strong Pipeline But Weak Profitability, Hold
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Based on a detailed revenue dissection and consideration of uncertainties surrounding federal and state infrastructure funding. I would like to recommend a Hold rating for Tutor Perini Corporation (NYSE:TPC) until the next quarterly report provides clearer visibility on its operating margins and infrastructure spending outlook. The company has recorded negative margin trends across two major business segments, raising concerns about its profitability. My FCFE model has implied a difference between Tutor Perini’s current market price ($64.50 per share as of 16 October 2025) and its intrinsic value ($37 - $43 per share). The model suggests the company has limited upside potential unless margin recovery materializes faster than expected.

Company Overview

Tutor Perini Corporation is a construction company, known for providing general contracting, pre-construction, project management, and design-build services to government, local agencies, and private customers. The corporation has acquired various business entities specializing in electrical, plumbing, mechanical, HVAC, and other services over the years, utilizing its vertical integration capabilities to further enhance its expertise in civil and building construction projects.

Strong Backlog Provides Revenue Visibility

In the latest earnings call, Tutor Perini’s CEO, Gary, announced the major upcoming project opportunities, including the $12 billion Sepulveda Transit Corridor, the $3.8 billion Southeast Gateway Line, the $1.2 billion Valley Link Phase 1 rail project, the $650 million Foothill Gold Line light rail project, and the $1.4 billion I-535 Blatnik Bridge project in Minnesota. These major projects are worth a total of $19.05 billion, and the management expects 1 or 2 of them to land.

Tutor Perini’s revenue is categorized under 3 main business segments: civil, building, and specialty contractors. All 3 segments have recorded a long-term positive backlog trend over the past 3 fiscal years. Furthermore, Tutor Perini had achieved outstanding average backlog QoQ growth rates over the past year (27.76%, 14.48%, and 13.70% for the civil, building, and specialty contractors’ segments, respectively).

Tutor Perini’s civil segment continues to benefit from strong demand in transportation projects, infrastructure improvement projects, and public agencies' long-term spending plans, and its building segment will be further enhanced by various ongoing healthcare and education projects in California; the anticipated value is not fully included yet in the current reported backlog. Notably, on 10 September 2025, the company’s subsidiary, Rudolph and Sletten, had been awarded a $960 million UCSF Benioff Children’s Hospital project contract, and the project’s value will likely be incorporated into Tutor Perini’s backlog in the third quarter of 2025.

Aside from the $960 million contract, Tutor Perini has announced several major new awards in September 2025, including a $41.9 million utility systems repair project, an electrical subcontract for a standby power generator under the Passaic Valley Sewerage Commission, a $181.8 million contract for the Guam Defense System, a $19.7 million I-41 Bridges project, and a $60.2 million modernization effort at Rothschild Dam over the past month. These recent awards are expected to be reflected in the upcoming quarter’s backlog, indicating a steady growth in the company’s backlog.

Another key strength of Tutor Perini is its consistent positive quarterly backlog burn rate. Alongside the increment of backlog, Tutor Perini also had a constant upward trend in the revenue figures, especially a significant increment in civils’ profit over the past two quarters, primarily supported by the increased execution of newer, higher-margin projects.

Federal Budget Headwinds And Customer Concentration Risk

Undeniably, the significant backlog figures and the constant revenue growth rate have shown potential upside of Tutor Perini, but if we deep dive into the composition of backlog, revenue, and income from operations margin, which is the real profit from Tutor Perini’s operation, a few concerns will keep me from investing in it for now.

In Trump’s discretionary budget request for fiscal year 2026, the base non-defence discretionary budget (including the Department of Education and the Department of Health and Human Services) has been suggested to be reduced significantly, including cuts in spending on the National Institute of Health, strengthening education institutions, institutional reforms, and many healthcare and education-related funding programs. The purpose of the proposed funding budget is to transfer the costs from the federal government to the states and local communities, further increasing financial pressure and tightening budget constraints on state and local agencies.

The Trump administration’s recent policy direction and the stricter federal funding reviews introduce uncertainties that may reduce future federal support for Tutor Perini’s infrastructure projects. For example, the Trump administration has reallocated $2.4 billion of funds from California’s high-speed rail program to other railroad partnership programs. Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy has warned agencies that there is a risk of losing federal support if they fail to strengthen safety measures. The reduction of federal funding may affect Tutor Perini’s project cash flows and, in turn, affect contractors over time.

Amid tightening budget constraints on state and local agencies, one of the major concerns will be that the majority of Tutor Perini's revenue comes from the state and local agencies and federal agencies. Approximately 74% of Tutor Perini’s revenue in the most recent quarter was derived from state and local agencies and federal agencies. This revenue composition has imposed a concentration risk. The budgetary pressures won’t significantly cause cancellation of the backlog projects, but could lead to delays in project execution, slower funding approvals, and eventually increase the cost of operations and further reduce the margin.

Negative Operating Margins And Cost Overrun Risk

Next, the concentration risk and the fiscal tightening caused by the changes in the United States budget planning have further led to another concern of mine, which is the low operating margin ratio, especially on Tutor Perini’s building and specialty contractors’ segment. The company’s civil segment continues to maintain a positive operating margin and achieves a strong 19.08% margin for the three months ended 30 June 2025. However, the company’s profitability has been hugely dependent on the civil segment operations over the past two years. The historical average operating profit margin for the building and specialty contractors’ segment over the past 10 quarters is –3.71% and –19.14%, respectively. The fact that margins not only remained low but turned negative in certain periods is especially troubling.

Despite a huge backlog having been recorded, if Tutor Perini can’t improve its operating margin, the project backlogs will only convert to a burden. Slight improvements have been observed for the building segment over the past two quarters, but no significant improvement has been recorded for the specialty contractors’ operating margin. In the latest Form 10-Q, the specialty contractors’ segment has recorded another $18 million in operating losses, which were primarily due to the settlement of certain legacy claims in the Northeast. The incident can be said to be a non-recurring event, but operating losses in the specialty contractors’ segment have been recorded in each quarter over the past two fiscal years. I remain cautious about management’s assumption of achieving a 5% to 8% margin range for the specialty contractors’ segment in 2026. Note that in my FCFE model, I have assumed a negative operating margin for the specialty contractors segment based on the historical average margin as a conservative measure.

Besides that, there are huge operating losses on both the civil and building segments by late 2024, and they have been explained as a non-recurring event by Tutor Perini management. According to the latest Form 10-K, the civil segment’s operating profit has been adversely impacted by an unfavorable arbitration ruling tied to a legacy dispute concerning a complete bridge project in California, which cost the company $101.6 million. The building segment’s profit was also reduced by $25.9 million on a government building project in Florida. However, the unfavorable loss in the building segment’s operations is related to the increased cost associated with subcontractors and delayed orders during the project execution. I have treated both losses as one-off events to normalize the historical margin in my FCFE model, but the loss in building segment operations has shown a cautious sign on the project execution risk for me. It will be important to observe whether Tutor Perini can maintain a positive margin for its building segment in the next quarter to confirm that its improvement is sustainable.

Additionally, coupled with the low operating margin concern, Tutor Perini’s revenue composition shows a high proportion of fixed-price contracts, which is riskier as the company must complete the work within that price regardless of cost overruns. If there are any delays in project execution or inflation due to the new tariff policy, Tutor Perini is to absorb those costs under the fixed-price contracts, and they can’t be passed to the client. Given Tutor Perini’s history of low operating margin, even modest project delays or any unexpected site conditions could lead to further margin compression, which is another major concern for me. Thus, I will remain on hold from taking a position in the stock until the next quarterly earnings release provides clearer evidence of sustainable margin improvement.

Free Cash Flow To Equity (FCFE) Valuation Verdict: Numbers That Matter

Based on my three-stage FCFE model, the intrinsic value per share should range from $37 to $43 per share (using a weighted average diluted share count of 53,194,000 on 30 June 2025), which is significantly lower than the current market price ($64.50 per share as of 16 October 2025).

In my FCFE model, a conservative approach is applied by selecting the lower bound and realistic assumptions to reflect cautious expectations of Tutor Perini’s future performance. I have projected FCFE over the next 4 quarters, beginning with the operating income and incorporating non-cash adjustments, subtracting the fixed capital investment, and adjusting for the changes in working capital and net borrowing. To avoid short-term forward negative FCFE, which is mainly driven by timing and discrete items that are unlikely to persist at the same scale, I have calculated a normalized FCFE by averaging the next four quarters’ FCFE with the prior four quarters’ FCFE to smooth timing noise and one-offs.

For the terminal value calculation, the Gordon-Growth approach is used. The DuPont analysis has been used to calculate the estimated short-term growth rate (3.88%), and the long-term growth rate will be anchored to the long-run GDP growth (2.5%). A weighted average cost of capital of 10.5% will be used. Noted that the estimated FCFE growth rate is significantly lower than the revenue and backlog growth rate due to the company’s low profitability margin. I would recommend a Hold rating rather than initiating new positions. It is advisable to wait until the next quarterly results provide clearer confirmation of trends in operating margin, revenue trajectory, backlog, and revenue composition before considering any upward adjustments to the valuation assumptions.

FCFE Model Supplementary Assumptions

Starting from the first line of the financial model, the operating income has been used to exclude the non-operating income. For the non-cash adjustment, there’s a notable increase in share-based compensation over the past quarter, mainly driven by a sharp rise in the company’s share price, which impacted the fair value of liability-classified awards. Based on management’s guidance, the share-based compensation expense impact per diluted share is $1.97. This information has been used to forecast the next 2 quarters of share-based compensation expense.

According to the management’s latest guidance, the total capital expenditures for 2025 are anticipated to range between $140 million and $150 million. These figures are used to forecast the capital expenditures in my FCFE model. Additionally, the historical average of the CapEx to revenue ratio was used to enhance consistency. Nevertheless, the effective tax rate used in my FCFE model will be 28%, consistent with the management’s assumption mentioned in the latest earnings call.

For the net working capital forecast, several balance sheet items have been identified as non-working capital and excluded from my FCFE analysis. Based on Tutor Perini’s footnote, the restricted cash and restricted investments are primarily collateral to secure insurance-related contingent obligations and deposits. These are contractually restricted and not operating working capital. Besides that, in the statement footnote, the retention receivable of Tutor Perini included the investments in lieu of retention, which are similar to restricted collateral not considered operating working capital. Thus, I have adjusted and excluded these figures from my FCFE calculation.

Key Risks And Uncertainties

Building upon the previous discussion, the uncertainties surrounding the Trump administration’s new budget allocation and fiscal tightening challenges by the state and local agencies have a significant impact on Tutor Perini’s revenue trajectory in the near or mid-term future. Any policy that may increase the cost of operations (such as a new tariff policy) or reduce federal and state infrastructure funding represents a major downside risk to the future financial outlook.

Besides that, investors may need to take notes about the execution and cost overrun risks. Tutor Perini has recorded a constant low margin for the past two fiscal years, alongside a high percentage of fixed-price contracts in their backlog. Any incidents (such as scope revisions, unsuccessful or delayed claims, and legal disputes) that cause delays in future project execution could lead to costs exceeding revenues, thereby further compressing operating margins.

Conclusion

In summary, Tutor Perini’s profitability recovery trajectory is still unclear, and with margins still under pressure and valuation exceeding intrinsic estimates, I recommend holding the stock until the next quarter report shows clearer evidence of margin stabilization and earnings recovery.

data.title: Cybersecurity: From National Security To The Corporate Balance Sheet
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

October marks Cybersecurity Awareness Month, a timely backdrop for examining how digital defense now spans national security, corporate resilience and investor opportunity. The Heathrow disruption and rising supply chain attacks remind us: these layers aren't separate stories but one connected reality.

When people talk about cybersecurity, the conversation often fractures. One camp gravitates toward the macro story: hostile states, ransomware gangs, hospitals or pipelines going dark. The other focuses on the micro: endpoint software, firewalls and identity tools. Rarely are these perspectives stitched into one narrative. Yet, they belong together. Cybersecurity is simultaneously a matter of national strategy, corporate resilience and investor opportunity. And, in 2025, all three are colliding.

Even as cyberthreats mount globally, recent events at Heathrow illustrate how fragile critical infrastructure remains—even in tightly regulated and security-conscious locales. In September 2025, a cyber-related disruption that struck check-in and boarding systems at Heathrow (alongside airports in Berlin and Brussels) was traced back to a service provider, Collins Aerospace.1 Although no definitive attribution had been confirmed at the time, experts warned that the event underscores how vulnerabilities in third-party systems can ripple outward, creating national security, economic and reputational risks.

Cybercrime as National Security

It has become routine to call cyberspace the "fifth domain" of warfare. But behind the cliché lies a blunt reality: the distinction between crime and war is collapsing. In 2024, financially motivated attackers accounted for almost four times as many intrusions as state-backed groups.2 Yet whether a hospital is crippled by ransomware or a state actor's wiper malware, the effect is the same—patients wait, care is delayed, and lives are put at risk.

One study found in-hospital mortality spikes by 35%–41% during ransomware disruptions.3 These are not nuisance events. They are national security crises in disguise, bleeding out through the balance sheets of hospitals, logistics companies and critical infrastructure.

And the scale is staggering. A single ransomware attack forced 150 U.S. plasma donation centers offline. Another wave knocked 25 Romanian hospitals out of service. In the U.S., the FBI estimates that business email compromise alone has drained $55 billion from global firms since 2013.4 When such figures are stacked against the gross domestic product (GDP) of smaller nations, the point is clear: cybercrime is an economy-level risk.

The Criminal-State Nexus

Cybercrime is no longer a cottage industry. It is an ecosystem, with suppliers of stolen credentials, malware developers and "initial access brokers."5 In that marketplace, states shop alongside criminals.

Russia has drawn deeply on this ecosystem in its campaigns against Ukraine and NATO6 countries. GRU7-linked APT448 has redeployed ransomware variants purchased from criminal forums. North Korea flips the model: its hackers generate revenue directly for the regime, stealing $3 billion in cryptocurrency between 2017 and 2023. Iran and China blur the lines further, embedding ransomware or extortion inside espionage campaigns, partly to confuse attribution.9

This is the murky zone that policymakers and companies must now navigate. The same malware kit can power a petty heist one week and an assault on critical infrastructure the next. The neat division between crime and geopolitics no longer holds.

Policy Playing Catch-Up

Governments have been forced to respond, though the pace and style vary across geographies.

  • Europe has gone the furthest. The NIS2 Directive, effective in 2023, expands obligations across sectors: telecoms, social media, cloud services and even public administrations. It harmonizes enforcement, imposes strict reporting timelines and explicitly addresses supply chain risks.10
  • The UK raised the stakes in 2025 with its Cyber Security and Resilience Bill. Regulators can now designate "critical suppliers," pull smaller firms into scope if they are pivotal and require incident notifications within 24 hours. In emergencies, the government can even direct companies to act.11
  • The U.S., by contrast, has been more piecemeal. CISA12 sets baselines, and both administrations have invested in supply chain resilience. But compared with Europe's centralized, punitive approach, the U.S. patchwork looks softer.

For businesses, these differences matter. In Europe, compliance is non-negotiable and costly. In the U.S., adoption follows breaches rather than mandates. For investors, that means uneven demand curves: a steady regulatory bid in Europe, more volatile spending cycles in America.

Cybersecurity as Growth Infrastructure

Too often, cybersecurity is framed as a drag—a necessary but unproductive cost. That framing is increasingly obsolete. Cybersecurity is growth infrastructure.

The UK government was explicit: "There is no growth without stability."13 That isn't political rhetoric; it's basic economics. A ransomware attack that halted customs in Costa Rica paralyzed trade, causing losses measured in millions of dollars per day.14 Attacks on cloud providers cascade into lost productivity across industries.

Secure digital infrastructure is to the 21st century what ports and highways were to the 20th. It underpins innovation, attracts investment and makes possible the layering of new technologies. Without resilient networks, AI adoption, internet of things (IoT) expansion and cloud migration stall. The fastest way to derail innovation is to ignore defense.

Where Companies Fit

The corporate layer translates policy and threat into solutions. A snapshot of leading firms illustrates the ecosystem:

  • Edge defenders: Examples include Cloudflare, Akamai and Fastly—guarding the internet's outer perimeter against distributed denial of service (DDoS) attacks and traffic manipulation.
  • Network security leaders: Examples include Zscaler, Palo Alto, Fortinet and Check Point—designing cloud firewalls and zero-trust architectures.
  • Endpoint sentinels: Examples include CrowdStrike, SentinelOne and Trend Micro—monitoring devices and servers for intrusions.
  • Observability and resilience: Examples include Datadog, Elastic and Commvault—tracking anomalies, ensuring data availability.
  • Data protectors: Examples include Rubrik, NetApp and Varonis—specialists in backup, recovery and ransomware resilience.
  • Identity guardians: Examples include CyberArk and Okta—securing the digital keys attackers covet.

Each slice of the stack lines up with the threats and regulations. Supply chain security mandates create demand for identity and monitoring. Ransomware pressures fuel adoption of backup and recovery. AI-driven threats magnify the need for edge filtering in real time. What looks like a fragmented vendor universe is, in fact, a map of how risks manifest.

Economics of Cybersecurity

From an economic lens, cybersecurity spending looks less like consumer tech and more like defense. It is countercyclical. Breaches trigger spending spikes. Regulation hardwires baseline demand. Geopolitical events reset urgency.

Verizon's 2025 report found that attacks via third parties rose nearly 15% in a single year. Each percentage point isn't abstract—it represents incremental procurement budgets, board-level urgency and software adoption curves. Marks & Spencer learned this the hard way in 2025, when an attack on a supplier spilled into its systems.15

That urgency explains why the global cybersecurity market—already $150 billion in 2022—is forecast to more than double by 2026.16 This is not discretionary software. It is insurance for the digital economy.

Investor Dilemmas

But opportunity comes with complexity. Three dilemmas stand out:

  • Fragmentation vs. consolidation: Enterprises often juggle dozens of tools. That creates inefficiency but also a chance for platforms like Palo Alto to consolidate. Specialists, however, remain critical in fast-evolving niches.
  • Regulatory divergence: The EU and UK provide stable but costly growth. The U.S. is larger but unpredictable, oscillating between neglect and crisis-driven surges.
  • The paradox of success: Cybersecurity works best when invisible. Success means breaches avoided—an absence that can make costs seem excessive until the next disaster validates them.

Investors have to think like insurers: pricing risk in a world where both regulation and criminal ingenuity are moving targets.

Closing: The Invisible Backbone

Cybersecurity is an invisible backbone of modern economies. It is national security when hospitals are locked. It is economic stability when customs systems go down. It is corporate strategy when boards weigh cloud migration. And it is investment when portfolios allocate capital to the firms building resilience.

The defining challenge of 2025 is that this backbone is under continuous strain. The weave between crime, state, policy and corporate defense is tight—and tightening. To understand cybersecurity, you cannot isolate the layers. You have to see the whole.

And seeing the whole reveals a truth that should shape strategy, policy and investment alike: cybersecurity is not simply protection. It is the infrastructure on which everything else depends.

Cybersecurity Awareness Month reinforces what 2025 makes clear: defense, policy and investment are converging. From boardrooms to battlefields, resilience isn't a side cost—it's the infrastructure that underpins growth, stability and innovation.

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal. The Fund invests in cybersecurity companies, which generate a meaningful part of their revenue from security protocols that prevent intrusion and attacks to systems, networks, applications, computers and mobile devices. Cybersecurity companies are particularly vulnerable to rapid changes in technology, rapid obsolescence of products and services, the loss of patent, copyright and trademark protections, government regulation and competition, both domestically and internationally. Cybersecurity company stocks, especially those that are internet-related, have experienced extreme price and volume fluctuations in the past that have often been unrelated to their operating performance. These companies may also be smaller and less experienced companies, with limited product or service lines, markets or financial resources and fewer experienced management or marketing personnel. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit, and the Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index or take defensive positions in declining markets. The composition of the Index is heavily dependent on quantitative and qualitative information and data from one or more third parties, and the Index may not perform as intended. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.
